name: Tonatiuh
description: Tonatiuh, in Aztec mythology, was the sun god of the Aztec empire. He was considered the leader of Tollan and the fifth and current sun. According to the Aztec creation myth, the world had gone through four previous suns, each destroyed by cataclysm. Tonatiuh demanded human sacrifice to sustain him and ensure the sun's daily journey across the sky. Without this nourishment, the sun would fail to rise, plunging the world into eternal darkness. He is often depicted as a fierce warrior, adorned with eagle feathers and holding a shield. His face is typically red, symbolizing the heat and energy of the sun. Tonatiuh's role was crucial for the survival of the Aztec people, as he provided light, warmth, and life-giving energy. He was closely associated with warfare and sacrifice, reflecting the Aztec belief that human blood was necessary to maintain cosmic order. The god's image was prominently featured on the Aztec calendar stone, emphasizing his central importance in their cosmology. Tonatiuh was believed to reside in the heavens during the day and journey through the underworld at night, battling forces of darkness before emerging again at dawn. He embodies the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, and is a powerful symbol of the Aztec worldview.
subtitle: The Aztec Sun God of the Fifth Sun

keyMyths.title: The Fifth Sun
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh became the fifth sun after Nanahuatzin sacrificed himself in a fire. This act brought light to the world, initiating the current era.

keyMyths.title: Daily Journey
keyMyths.description: Tonatiuh traverses the sky each day, requiring human blood to maintain his strength. Without sacrifice, he would fail and the world would end.

keyMyths.title: Underworld Passage
keyMyths.description: At night, Tonatiuh journeys through the underworld, battling forces of darkness. This journey is perilous and requires assistance from other deities.
